data.reasoning: Both models skipped the only reviewed file (PKGBUILD) due to a tooling bug that misclassified the PKGBUILD as a binary file. No actual content was analyzed by either model, so no safety determination can be made. The verdict must be inconclusive pending a proper review.
data.disagreement_analysis: There is no substantive disagreement. Both models produced identical outcomes: a 0-file review caused by the same tooling error that flagged PKGBUILD as a binary file and skipped it.
coverage_issues: PKGBUILD was never reviewed — it was incorrectly classified as a binary file and skipped by both models
No .install hooks, source files, or supplementary scripts were enumerated or reviewed
Zero files reviewed across all reports; complete absence of coverage
false_negatives: Entire PKGBUILD contents unreviewed — for a JetBrains EAP JRE package this typically involves downloading remote binaries/tarballs (curl/wget of versioned URLs), checksum validation, and package() installation steps that need verification
Any .install scripts (common for JRE/IDE packages to set up paths or symlinks) were not detected or reviewed
re_audit_focus: PKGBUILD — verify source URLs, download integrity (sha256sums vs SKIP), and build/package functions
Source array entries for remote fetches and any executed prepare/build steps
learnings: The binary-file detection heuristic is misclassifying plain-text PKGBUILD files as binary, aborting the entire audit. PKGBUILD should be explicitly whitelisted as text and always reviewed regardless of binary heuristics.
A skipped PKGBUILD should hard-fail the run with a distinct error code rather than producing a 'No issues found' style summary, which could be misread as benign.
When the initial PKGBUILD safety check cannot run, the tool should not proceed/terminate silently; it should surface a clear actionable error and avoid emitting confusing 'No issues found' text.
Consider charset/encoding sniffing improvements (e.g., null-byte presence rather than non-ASCII bytes) to reduce false binary classification of UTF-8 shell scripts.
